data.title: Investing In Platinum And Palladium After The Correction With The SPPP ETF Product
data.description:
The two leading platinum group metals, platinum and palladium, had lagged their precious cousins, gold and silver, for years. Palladium reached a record high of $3,3425 per ounce in early 2022 before plunging below the $1,000 level. Platinum’s record high was in 2008 at $2308.80 per ounce. Platinum spent nearly a decade, from 2015 through Q1 2025, trading around a $1,000 per ounce pivot point.

Platinum and palladium broke out to the upside in Q2 2025, reversing the trends from the previous years. The last time I covered the Sprott Physical Platinum and Palladium Trust (NYSEARCA:SPPP) on Seeking Alpha was on October 16, 2024, when I concluded:

Adding SPPP to portfolios is a value approach to precious metals that could pay off over the coming months and years if the currency consolidation gives way to bullish trends in the thinly traded precious metals.

Nearby NYMEX platinum futures were trading at $1,007.80 per ounce on October 16, 2024, while the nearby NYMEX palladium futures were at $1,019 per ounce. The SPPP ETF was at the $10.04 per share level. Prices of the metals and SPPP have moved higher since October 2024.

Technical breakouts in Platinum and Palladium in Q2 2025

Platinum reached a record high of $2,308.80 in 2008. Since 2015, platinum traded around a $1,000 per ounce pivot point until Q2 2025.

The quarterly chart highlights the NYMEX platinum futures $909.40 to $1,063.80 trading range over the first three months of 2025. In Q2, the range was from $878.30 to $1,440.50 per ounce, with platinum settling Q2 at $1,343 on June 30, 2025. Platinum fell below the Q1 low in Q2 and settled above the high from Q1, forming a bullish key reversal pattern on the long-term chart. While platinum was trading around the $1,380 level in early September, the price rose to a high of $1,511.40 in Q3, the highest price since 2014.

The quarterly chart highlights the NYMEX palladium futures trading range of $901.00 to $1,077.50 over the first quarter of 2025. In Q2, the range was from $870.50 to $1,193.50 per ounce, with palladium settling Q2 at $1,107.10 on June 30, 2025. Like platinum, palladium fell below the Q1 low in Q2 and settled above the high from Q1, forming a bullish key reversal pattern on the long-term chart. While palladium was trading around the $1,145 level in early September, the price rose to a higher $1,373.50 high in Q3, which was the highest price since Q2 2023.

The bullish case for the rare platinum group metals

Platinum and palladium futures broke out to the upside in Q2 2025. The factors supporting higher prices include:

  • Platinum and palladium are rare precious metals, with annual mine supplies of 180 and 190 metric tons, respectively.
  • Russia and South Africa produce 75% of the precious metals, with South Africa leading in platinum output and Russia slightly edging out South Africa in palladium production.
  • The density and high melting points of the platinum group metals make them indispensable for a wide range of industrial applications.
  • Compared to gold and silver futures markets, platinum and palladium are far less liquid.

As of September 3, 2025:

  • COMEX gold futures open interest was 494,776 contracts. At $3,615 per ounce, the gold futures market value was above $178.86 billion.
  • COMEX silver futures open interest was 158,277 contracts. At $41.50 per ounce, the silver futures market value was above $32.84 billion.
  • The NYMEX platinum futures open interest stood at 89,968 contracts. At $1,380 per ounce, the platinum futures market value was above $6.20 billion.
  • The NYMEX palladium futures open interest stood at 18,687 contracts. At $1,145 per ounce, the palladium futures market value was just below $2.14 billion.

The bottom line is that less liquid markets often exhibit higher price variance, as bids to buy can disappear during selloffs and offers to sell may evaporate during rallies. Lower liquidity tends to result in significant price fluctuations. After putting in bullish key reversals on the long-term quarterly charts in Q2 2025, platinum and palladium could be in a position to experience further upside price action.

Gold and silver support higher prices

Gold and silver are in long-term bullish trends. Gold has been rising, making higher lows and higher highs since the 1999 bottom of $252.50 per ounce. Over the past eight consecutive quarters, the price of gold has increased to a new record high and was trading above the $3,600 per ounce level on September 4.

Silver’s path of least resistance has been bullish since the 2020 pandemic-inspired low of $11.64 per ounce. At over $41.40 per ounce in early September 2025, silver’s price is more than three and a half times higher than the 2020 low.

As market participants have turned to the security of precious metals over the past years, gold and silver have shone. With platinum over $900 per ounce below its all-time high from 2008 and palladium nearly $2,300 under its high from 2022, the platinum group metals trading on the CME’s NYMEX division offer significant value compared with gold and silver trading on the CME’s COMEX division. The bottom line is that the bullish price action in gold and silver, combined with reduced liquidity in platinum and palladium futures, could lead to parabolic rallies in the PGMs.

SPPP owns physical platinum and palladium

The fund summary for the Sprott Physical Platinum and Palladium Trust states:

As of September 3, 2025, SPPP held 168,124 ounces of platinum and 137,036 ounces of palladium. At $12.14 per share, SPPP had nearly $376 million in assets under management. SPPP trades an average of over 354,000 shares daily, making it a highly liquid ETF product. SPPP’s expense ratio is 1.05%.

No Seeking Alpha ETF Grades, but a bullish trend

Seeking Alpha does not provide ETF Grades for the SPPP ETF. However, the ETF that owns physical platinum and palladium has tracked the leading PGM prices in 2025.

Since the end of 2024:

  • Platinum at $1,380 per ounce was 51.6% higher than the 2024 closing price of $910.50.
  • Palladium at $1,145 per ounce was 25.9% higher than the 2024 closing price of $909.80.

The monthly chart highlights SPPP’s 36.6% rise from $8.89 at the end of last year to $12.14 on September 4, 2025, which reflects the average increases in platinum and palladium prices over the past eight months. SPPP reached a peak of $13.34 in July 2025, when platinum and palladium prices hit their respective highs.

Platinum and palladium prices have corrected from the July 2025 highs, which could be the perfect time to consider adding the metals to portfolios. Industrial and precious metals offer significant value compared to gold and silver in the current market environment. Moreover, a lack of liquidity in the PGM futures could lead to a parabolic rally over the coming months. The SPPP ETF owns physical platinum and palladium bullion and does an excellent job tracking the metals’ prices.

data.title: Ciena Q3: Today's Big Winner In The AI Buildout
data.description:
Introduction

When a stock has already more than doubled in a year, you usually assume the biggest moves are behind it. But then you get a moment like this, when Ciena Corporation (NYSE:CIEN) reports one of its strongest quarters in years and suddenly the stock surges another 20% in a single day, and you realize how wrong those assumptions can be.

Going into this quarter, expectations for Ciena were very low. Analysts had cut their estimates 14 times, and not a single one had raised them. That tells you how little confidence there was in the company’s near-term performance. The general belief was that after such a big run in the stock, growth was slowing down and that results would probably disappoint. What actually happened was the opposite, and this strong surprise acted as a catalyst for the stock jump. Ciena reported a very strong quarter while raising guidance and connected the growth to AI with tons of buzzwords to make sure people noticed them, and it worked.

A Quarter of Surprises

Ciena’s Q3 revenue came in at $1.22 billion, up almost 30% from last year. Earnings per share almost doubled to $0.67, beating the expectations by $0.14. Margins were stable enough. Gross margin decreased slightly from a year ago to 41.9%, but operating margin improved, with adjusted operating margin expanding to 10.7% from 8%.

It was really hard not to notice the cash flow, which exceeded all expectations. Last year, this business was burning cash, but this year, they not only fixed that, they showed great positive numbers. This quarter, it generated $174 million from operations and $135 million in free cash flow.

Talking about the drivers of growth, cloud demand has become the engine. Direct revenue from cloud providers surged 94% year-over-year and now makes up 40% of the total. Non-telco business overall crossed the halfway mark, accounting for 53% of revenue. And geographically, they are strong in various places. The growth is not concentrated: EMEA grew 38%, and APAC 24%.

For the past two years, everyone has been talking about how AI would transform demand for pretty much everything, but also high-speed networking, which correlates directly with Ciena’s demand. This quarter, the company showed what that looks like in practice. It booked its first revenue from WaveLogic 6 Nano pluggables, deployed WL6e with 11 new customers, including one of the top three hyperscalers, and set records in both WaveLogic 5 Nano and Routing/Switching shipments. Even the new WaveRouter had a milestone, trialing 1.6 Tbps in a router for the first time.

I am looking at this company for the first time, and if you are like me, the explanation of products can be helpful. If you followed it before, skip this paragraph. Ciena makes the equipment that helps move huge amounts of data much faster, and it’s especially needed now, when our progress depends on huge data volumes. The WaveLogic products are their special chips and modules that push more traffic through networks at faster speeds. The older WaveLogic 5 is still selling very well, while the new WaveLogic 6 is starting to sell to the big cloud companies. Routing and Switching are the devices that direct all that traffic so it doesn’t get stuck. And the WaveRouter is their newest router, which just proved it can handle record levels of data.

Guidance

As if the beat was not enough, the Q4 guidance was even better. Ciena expects revenue of $1.24 to $1.32 billion, which is above consensus at $1.21 billion. Gross margin should grow to the 42–43% range, and operating expenses will rise modestly as the company invests in growth, which is a green flag as well. Companies like that need money to continue innovating and delivering.

And part of the reason why the stock is up so much is that nobody expected this. The bar was so low, it was essentially underground with 14 downward revisions. When you combine that with a beat on both the top and bottom line and the guidance raise, the jumps like this one today happen.

Valuation and Reality Check

It’s fair to say Ciena is an expensive stock by traditional metrics. It trades at ~42x forward earnings, which is more than 80% overvalued compared to both the sector and to its own five-year average. But in situations like this, I like to look at valuation against growth by using the PEG ratio (price to earnings divided by growth). By this metric, it looks like the company trades with a 35% discount compared to the sector and more than 15% compared to its own five-year average. In other words, even if the valuation expanded rapidly, the growth expanded even more, so when adjusting the valuation to growth, the stock is 15% cheaper today than it was in the past five years on average.

So if you are an investor, or a potential investor, the main question isn’t whether Ciena is expensive today; it’s whether the growth can hold at the current levels and expand from here. If you believe the company is just getting started and that the demand will pick up even further, then you have your answer: the stock has at least 15% upside. If you think the growth will stall, it could mean a big downside.

Conclusion

What happened this quarter is that Ciena proved the AI demand story isn’t just about chips and data centers. There are other sectors, adjacent to AI, that will benefit from growing demand, and Ciena is one of the companies in those industries. The company just showed it can scale into that demand, generate cash, and expand margins, while no one expected it to happen (or at least, analysts did not).

The company is a stretch at current levels, but once again, the decision about the stock depends on your future assumptions. I would be interested to follow this story and to see where it goes, but I wouldn’t risk jumping in after this surge.

data.title: Guggenheim Floating Rate Strategies Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Review

  • The Fund (Institutional Class) returned 2.31 percent (2.51 percent gross) for the second quarter of 2025, compared to the S&P UBS Leveraged Loan Index return of 2.33 percent.
  • Credit selection continued to be strong, particularly in the consumer cyclical sectors and in B-rated credits broadly, and was the primary contribution to relative performance during the quarter. Idiosyncratic credit issues in the consumer non-cyclicals sector continued to weigh on performance and was the primary detractor. Equity positions owned as part of restructurings were marked down and also detracted from performance during the period.

Strategy and Positioning

  • The overhang of tariff uncertainty and conflict in the Middle East rattled markets in April to start the quarter, but markets snapped back, and loan performance was solid overall during the period. Despite positive performance year to date, we remain cautious given tail risk in the market and feel tariff uncertainty remains. With any volatility, spread widening could offer better relative value opportunities in the market, and our cash position remains mildly elevated.
  • Defaults ticked slightly lower than the previous quarter to 2.8 percent at the end of the second quarter, in line with the long-term average. Tail risk persists, and while we expect defaults and losses to remain manageable, there is a larger amount of uncertainty going forward and we believe defaults will climb higher from here.
  • We are monitoring market volatility and have moved the portfolio up in quality after an elevated period of credit sales during the quarter. We look to take advantage of any volatility where loans may move lower, picking up higher quality credits at discounted prices. We remain selective in adding new names to the platform, with our new issue approval rate at 24 percent year-to-date, net of incremental term loans and refinancing transactions for existing credits.

Economic and Market Review

  • After initial tariff-related volatility roiled through the market, April returns ended the month only slightly negative as risk appetite returned quickly. May saw a significant rebound and June followed up with another solid positive return. Loans continued to experience volatility around policy/tariff headlines, but any negative price movements in loans were followed by a swift rebound.
  • The average price of the S&P UBS Leveraged Loan Index rose from $95.84 in the first quarter to $96.48 in the second quarter. The index’s average yield to maturity (YTM) declined roughly 58 basis points to 8.1 percent, while base rates remained unchanged.
  • After a short hiatus on new deal activity in April, the second quarter saw $80 billion of new issuance (excluding repricings), although overall 2025 volume lags 2024 by 23 percent. LBO and M&A volume does remain higher though year over year, with $52 billion in the first quarter and $33 billion in the second quarter, up 74 percent and 26 percent respectively year over year, although these tallies remain low based on long-term averages. Repricing activity slowed significantly from the first quarter after a record January, with second quarter printing just $28.5 billion of this type of spread-lowering transactions, the lowest quarterly tally since fourth quarter 2023.
  • The technical backdrop continues to support loan prices as demand outweighs supply. Loan demand was solid as collateralized loan obligation (CLO) issuance remained robust through the quarter at $51 billion, roughly in line with the previous four-quarter average (in a record year). Retail fund flows did weigh a bit on overall demand figures, at -$8.5 billion for the quarter. Combining measurable demand and supply, the technical picture continues to be in a demand surplus, despite a very brief supply surplus at the end of the first quarter during the tariff-related volatility.

data.title: ZIM Integrated: Best Time To Buy Is When Things Appear Bad (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
ZIM stock: Q2 earnings report faced strong headwinds

I last covered ZIM Integrated Shipping Services Ltd. (NYSE:ZIM) on July 15 with an article titled “ZIM Integrated: Dividend Cushion Supports Holding.” The analysis was triggered by its latest dividend declaration at that time and ended with a Hold rating on the stock. Since then, the company has released its earnings report (ER) for FQ2 2025 on August 20. The ER revealed a few important headwinds worthy of a reexamination, and this reexamination led us to upgrade our holding to Buy. A deciding consideration behind our upgrade is that if we want to buy cyclical stocks, it is best to buy them when things are at their worst.

Judging by the FQ2 financials and also the management’s outlook, I feel things are near their worst for ZIM. As you can see from the following chart, its profit suffered substantial declines across all metrics in the past quarter due to lower freight rates and lower shipping volume. These headwinds could persist longer due to the various ongoing geopolitical risks (which I will elaborate on later). But I considered that A) these issues are ultimately temporary and B) these headwinds have already been reflected in its current valuation with a FWD P/E of only around 4x. Further, I expect the dividend payouts to provide a key support for its stock prices as its operations recover, as detailed next.

ZIM stock: dividend in focus

As a direct reflection of the profit headwinds, ZIM announced a dividend of $0.06 per share for its Q3 payout, as seen in its ER slide below. A sharp decrease from the $0.74 per share paid in Q2 and $0.84 per share paid in Q3 2024.

However, out of all this bad news, ZIM announced an increase in its FY25 adjusted EBITDA and EBIT guidance to the range of $1.8-2.2 billion and $550-950 million, respectively. Under this guidance range and ZIM’s projected tax rates, the EPS estimate for its full fiscal year 2025 is around $3 per share. Following ZIM’s quarterly payout of 30%, investors could still expect an annual payout of around $1 per share going forward. This projected payout translates into an annualized FWD yield of around 7.4% at its current stock prices, providing sizable downside protection.

ZIM stock: dividend cushion ratio remains healthy

Furthermore, I see large odds for its dividend payouts to exceed the above projection for various reasons. The top reason on our list is our outlook for its profits to recover in the near future as geopolitical risks dissipate (more on this later). The second reason is that we are still seeing very healthy dividend coverage despite the ongoing headwinds.

Our projection of a $1 per share payout is based on a payout ratio of 30%. For reasons detailed in our last article, such a simple payout ratio completely ignores other key financials updated in its ER, such as the status of its liquidity, debts, and other financial obligations. To form a holistic assessment of its dividend coverage, we have found the following dividend cushion ratio (“DCR”) quite insightful. The concept originated from Brian M. Nelson’s book entitled Value Trap. To our knowledge, the key steps are quoted below for ease of reference.

The DCR is obtained by A) summing the existing net cash a company has on its balance sheet plus its expected future free cash flows over the next five years, and B) dividing that sum by future expected cash dividends (including expected growth in them, where applicable) over the same time period. If the ratio is significantly above 1, the company generally has sufficient financial capacity to pay out its expected future dividends. The higher the ratio, the better, all else equal.

With these steps, the final results are shown in the next chart. As seen, ZIM has been maintaining a DCR well above the 1x threshold in recent years, with an average DCR of 2.31x between 2022 and TTM 2025. Based on the latest financials and guidance provided for 2025, my projection for ZIM’s FWD DCR is about 1.91x. This admittedly reflects a deterioration of its dividend safety, but note that A) it is still far above 1x, and B) it reflects a deterioration far milder than what the accounting earnings would suggest in our view. The reason is that other changes in its financials helped to counterbalance the decrease in its accounting earnings.

For example, as you can see from its balance sheet below, its total liability for Q2 has decreased to about $7.19 billion compared to about $7.52 billion in the previous quarter.

Other risks and final thoughts

Now, back to the headwinds. In Q2 2025, ZIM faced a highly volatile shipping market. The company reported disruptions from U.S. tariff announcements, along with fluctuating demand, freight rates, and a relatively weak transpacific market. Looking ahead, I expect these challenges to persist longer. In particular, the reshuffling of ZIM’s capacity due to changes in U.S.-China tariff discussions could continue, and the tariff rates of 50% that President Donald Trump recently announced for India could add new disruptions.

However, despite these challenges, I see a skewness in ZIM’s return/risk profile. As mentioned earlier, I first believe these issues have already been priced in at its current prices and valuation multiples. The stock is only trading at a low P/E but is also heavily discounted from its book value, as seen in the next chart.

Management also mentioned the potential of its modern fleet to improve cost structure and their commitment to consistent dividend payout. As a reflection of their confidence, they updated the 2025 guidance range and increased its midpoints. Their comments are consistent with my own assessment, as shown above. As such, I feel optimistic that the company can either deliver or even exceed the updated guidance range.

To close, this follow-up on ZIM stock was triggered by its latest FQ2 ER and also the market response following the ER. The market has good reasons for a selloff given the strong headwinds observed in Q2. I believe the selloff has fully reflected the negatives already at this point. In particular, my model still shows robust dividend payouts going forward and an attractive yield, providing a substantial cushion against further share price corrections. These considerations have led me to adjust my rating to buy under current conditions.

data.title: Diamond Hill Long-Short Strategy Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Commentary

Following a sharp downturn in April in the wake of President Trump’s “Liberation Day” tariffs announcement, markets rose relatively uniformly for the rest of the quarter. For the quarter, US stocks rose approximately 11% (as measured by the Russell 3000 Index). Large-cap stocks bounced the most in the quarter, rising just over 11%, while small and mid caps gained roughly 8.5% each. From a style perspective, growth again outperformed value, which, given the sharp market rebound and seeming improvement in investor sentiment, was not particularly surprising. Large-cap growth stocks rose nearly 18% in the quarter as big technology stocks resumed market leadership; in contrast, large-cap value stocks increased by a more modest 3.8%. Likewise, mid-cap growth stocks were up by more than 18% compared to mid-cap value stocks, which rose by a little over 5%. The gap between small-cap growth and value was the smallest, at +12% versus +5%, respectively.

From a sector perspective, investors’ risk-on attitude seemed to likewise return, with the growthier sectors in the Russell 1000 Index leading, while defensive sectors fell out of favor. Information technology stocks performed the best, rising over 23%, followed by communication services, which advanced 19%. Energy (-7.7%), health care (-6.3%) and real estate (-0.5%) were the only sectors in the red in Q2. Energy was hindered by geopolitical upheaval, about which we’ll discuss shortly.

2Q25 Russell 1000 Index Sector Returns (%)

As has largely been the case since 2025 began, economic data appear to have a minimal impact on market movements, in contrast to headlines surrounding ostensible winners and losers in the ongoing trade upheaval that has prevailed since President Trump took office in January. In Q2, following Trump’s announced raft of tariffs, many countries sought new trade deals with the US, and several reached significant new agreements, including the UK and China. The US and China agreed in May to cut tariffs on one another for the ensuing 90 days. Since then, progress with China seems to have largely stalled — likewise, progress on a new US-EU deal. However, neither the US nor China has reverted to the prior threatened tariff rates, which were inarguably exorbitant. Meanwhile, bilateral discussions between other countries — including the UK and India and the UK and the EU — seem to have borne fruit, the former trade partners reaching an agreement roughly three years after beginning negotiations and the latter agreeing to reset post-Brexit negotiations, ideally clearing the way for tangible progress toward a lasting trade deal.

In contrast to the market’s reaction, central banks globally have seemingly remained committed to watching and reacting to economic data, with the Federal Reserve holding rates steady in June even amid pressure from President Trump and growing dissension among its ranks regarding whether a rate cut may be appropriate. The Bank of England likewise held rates steady in June. The European Central Bank (ECB), however, cut rates again in June, even as inflation fell below its 1.9% target, though ECB President Christine Lagarde indicated that rate cuts may be at an end.

If anything could steal headlines from all the quarter’s trade developments, it was geopolitics — and indeed, the ongoing Middle East war and the US’s increased role in it were front and center in June. Toward the end of the month, following the expiration of a 60-day window during which Iran could have returned to the negotiating table, President Trump authorized military strikes on three nuclear sites in the country. Iran subsequently launched retaliatory missile strikes against Israeli targets and fired several missiles at a US airbase in Qatar, though the latter missiles had little impact and resulted in no US casualties. Shortly after the US strikes, Trump announced a ceasefire between Iran and Israel and attempted to bring the two sides to the table — efforts which are ongoing as we write, though the truce appears to be holding for now. Meanwhile, the war between Russia and Ukraine continues apace, with the two sides currently seeming as far as they’ve been from either a ceasefire or meaningful progress toward a negotiated peace.

While the selloff in early Q2 created some interesting and compelling investment opportunities, it was undoubtedly a short window in which to capitalize, given the market’s sharp rebound. With equity markets returning to above- average valuations, it is challenging to believe that future returns will match historical averages over the next five years. However, we remain focused on our investment philosophy, which seeks to identify opportunities to invest in high-quality companies trading at valuations we believe are disconnected from their underlying fundamentals and long- term growth outlooks. We believe that by focusing in this way, we can continue to add value for our clients in the period ahead, regardless of how markets evolve from here.

Performance Discussion

Our portfolio trailed the Russell 1000 Index and the blended benchmark (60% Russell 1000 Index/40% Bloomberg US Treasury Bills 1-3 Month Index). Our long book posed a relative headwind in the quarter, particularly due to our below-benchmark exposure to the index’s top-performing information technology sector. Our long industrials holdings trailed benchmark peers, weighing on relative returns. Conversely, our long financials holdings provided a modest relative tailwind, as did our short book, which underperformed the index.

On an individual holdings basis, top contributors to return in Q2 were all from our long book, including Microsoft (MSFT) and Meta Platforms (META). Software and information technology services provider Microsoft’s Azure platform experienced reaccelerating revenue growth in Q2, and the company experienced double-digit overall revenue growth and expanding operating margins. We believe Microsoft is uniquely positioned in cloud, application software and artificial intelligence (AI) to generate continued revenue and operating profit growth in the period ahead. Shares of social media platform Meta Platforms are benefiting from solid advertising revenue growth, increased profitability and new initiatives within the AI space.

Other top Q2 contributors included our long positions in Citigroup (C), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSMC) (TSM) and Ulta Beauty (ULTA). Shares of banking and financial services company Citigroup rose as the company continues returning capital to shareholders, with the pace of buybacks increasing in Q2. Semiconductor manufacturer TSMC is benefiting from increased demand for its leading-edge semiconductor manufacturing technology from machine learning and cloud-computing applications. Specialty beauty retailer Ulta saw its first positive in-store comparable sales growth in over a year, giving a boost to investor sentiment around the company’s market share and competitive positioning.

Among our bottom individual contributors in Q2 were our short positions in Royal Caribbean (RCL) and International Business Machines (IBM). Shares of cruise line operator Royal Caribbean rose as concerns about the macroeconomic environment subsided and demand for leisure cruises remained robust. While the business is economically sensitive, Royal Caribbean has effectively capitalized on a favorable demand environment to drive solid underlying fundamentals.

Shares of diversified information technology company IBM rose amid ongoing investor enthusiasm about the company’s AI potential. Investors also seemingly consider IBM a defensive holding, which has given a boost to shares amid recent market volatility.

Other bottom individual contributors in Q2 included our short positions in Federal Signal Corporation (FSS) and Core & Main (CNM), as well as our long position in Becton, Dickinson. Shares of specialty vehicles manufacturer Federal Signal rose amid stronger-than-expected order trends. Shares of Core& Main — a US distributor of water, wastewater, storm drainage and fire protection products and services — rose during the quarter despite further evidence of weakening fundamentals, including contracting earnings and margins and slowing M&A-related growth. We consequently believe the company remains overvalued. Becton, Dickinson (BDX), a global medical technology company, declined as management anticipated tariffs would weigh on its fundamental outlook in the period ahead.

Portfolio Activity

Despite markets’ relatively sharp bounce following April’s downward volatility, we were able to initiate two new long positions in the quarter, Aon (AON) and Adobe (ADBE), as well as a few short positions, including Medpace Holdings (MEDP), MP Materials (MP) and Williams-Sonoma (WSM).

Aon is a global leader in insurance brokerage and consulting. We like the insurance brokerage business given its oligopolistic nature with high barriers to entry and switching costs, minimal capital requirements and an attractive growth profile. Aon’s 2024 acquisition of NFP is expected to continue creating value, with revenue synergy opportunities arising as Aon cross-sells products and services to the NFP client base. Furthermore, Aon is led by a solid management team with a track record of allocating capital to its highest return-on-invested-capital opportunities, including share repurchases. With shares pressured in Q2 as investors hoped for better near-term guidance, we capitalized on the opportunity to initiate a position at what we consider an attractive valuation.

Adobe is the largest provider of creative content software by a wide margin, providing a robust suite of tools used by design professionals in many verticals, such as graphic designers, video editors, and web and mobile app creators. The company also has strong positions in marketing and direct customer engagement software and owns the near- ubiquitous Adobe Acrobat platform. Though investors have seemingly weighed the potential for greater competition and AI disruption in the period ahead, we believe the current valuation largely reflects those concerns, particularly given the breadth and diversification of Adobe’s solutions, its incumbency and strong positioning in the market and its ongoing willingness to innovate — for example, over the last few years, it has infused more AI into its existing products. Consequently, we believe Adobe can deliver solid fundamentals over the next several years, and so we capitalized on what we consider an attractive discount relative to our estimate of intrinsic value to initiate a position in Q2.

Medpace is a late-stage contract research organization (CRO) providing outsourcing services primarily for Phase 2 and Phase 3 clinical trials. The company benefited from the 2021 biotech funding upcycle, which contributed to significant earnings growth over the ensuing couple of years. However, Medpace currently faces structural and cyclical headwinds — including a normalized competitive environment; double-digit award declines; industry pricing pressure and uncertainty around National Institutes of Health (NIH) funding cuts, most favored nation drug pricing, and Health & Human Services (HHS) and Federal Drug Administration (FDA) staffing reductions — which we anticipate will result in negative growth by 2026. Given these headwinds, combined with a high valuation, we believe Medpace represents an attractive short opportunity given our estimate of normalized earnings per share, which is meaningfully lower than the market consensus.

MP Materials is a US-based rare earth oxide (REO) producer. Though shares received a boost in Q2 amid escalating US-China trade tensions, fundamentals have deteriorated: the company has suspended all shipments of REO (which represents a significant majority of its revenue) to China and is transitioning from that slightly profitable business to the unprofitable business of separating neodymium-praseodymium (NdPr) from REO. It is also trying to ramp up another unprofitable venture, neodymium-iron-boron (NdFeB) magnets, which has been a zero-margin business for even the largest producers globally outside of China. Given the company’s high valuation relative to these fundamental developments, we find it an attractive short opportunity and initiated a position in Q2.

Williams-Sonoma is an omnichannel specialty retailer of home products. We believe the company will be unable to maintain its current profitability levels under a normalized demand environment. Furthermore, given the maturity of its core portfolio of brands, we don’t anticipate that Williams-Sonoma will be able to accelerate comparable sales growth meaningfully; consequently, we believe it represents an attractive short opportunity.

We exited our long position in mobile network carrier Verizon Communications (VZ) to redeploy our capital to more attractive opportunities. We also covered our short position in Carvana (CVNA) as shares reached our estimate of intrinsic value in favor of more attractive short opportunities.

The portfolio’s net exposure at the end of the quarter was 56%.

Market Outlook

Equity markets have demonstrated remarkable resilience in 2025, navigating a complex environment shaped by shifting policies, economic uncertainty and evolving investor sentiment. As we look ahead, the interplay between these factors will likely continue to influence market dynamics, presenting both challenges and opportunities for investors.

Valuations have returned to elevated levels, and while near-term economic growth projections remain healthy, signs of a weakening consumer have emerged across various sectors. Given the consumer’s critical role in driving the economy, further softening could pose risks to the high-single-digit corporate earnings growth currently anticipated for the year. Monitoring these trends will be essential as we assess the sustainability of current market expectations.

Investor preferences have also shifted notably this year, reflecting the broader uncertainty in the economic and policy landscape. While defensive and value stocks gained favor earlier in the year, recent trends have highlighted renewed interest in growth-oriented and economically sensitive sectors. These shifts underscore the importance of maintaining a flexible and forward-looking approach to portfolio management, as market leadership may continue to evolve in response to changing conditions.

The selloff earlier this year created a brief but compelling window for investment opportunities, though the subsequent market rebound quickly closed that window. As equity markets return to above-average valuation levels, it becomes increasingly challenging to expect returns that align with historical averages over the next five years. However, we remain steadfast in our commitment to delivering value-added results for our clients. By focusing on high-quality companies with strong fundamentals and long-term growth potential, we believe we can achieve returns that exceed the broader market over time.

Looking forward, the path ahead will likely be shaped by a combination of economic resilience, policy developments and shifts in market dynamics. By staying focused on our disciplined investment process, we aim to navigate these complexities and position our portfolio for long-term success.

data.title: InPost: "Strong Buy" On Undervaluation And Strong Earnings
data.description:
InPost (OTCPK:INPOY) has lost 25% of its value since my Buy rating, making it one of my worst-performing picks. In my prior report, I noted that the company needs to show economies of scale to warrant a continued "Buy" rating. While the market has negatively responded to the company’s Q2 2025 earnings, I believe the results were good. In this report, I analyze the InPost Q2 2025 earnings and update my price target.

Quarrel Between Allegro and InPost Affects Volumes In Poland

During the second quarter, parcel volume grew 22.5%, translating to sales growth of 34.7% to PLN 3.53 billion. According to Reuters, weaker volume growth in Poland weighs on the share price. This was driven by the company’s main partner in Poland broadening their logistics options. This company is Allegro, and in a somewhat rare move, InPost also showed dissatisfaction with Allegro during the Q2 2025 InPost Earnings Call:

Now let's turn to the marketplace, where in Q2, we saw volumes take a bit of a breather. As you know, our main partner in Poland has recently decided to broaden their logistics options. We completely respect the fact that the large platforms want to diversify. We understand the approach. We just don't love the style. They announced it openly at the beginning of the year and started pushing to change consumer habits, tricking users into choosing other delivery companies against stated user preferences. In fact, last quarter, Allegro really stepped up their efforts trying to redirect parcels from InPost to their own Allegro OneBox.

Not a huge surprise. But if you look at the social media in Poland, you will see plenty of frustrated customers. At one point, we estimate that even 30% of our Allegro parcels came with a suggestion to change the delivery option, even when customers had clearly chosen InPost. First of all, we believe this practice doesn't just bend the rules of our contract with Allegro, but is against the very basic consumer right to have a parcel delivered to the APM of their choice. That's why we have started legal action to stop it.

The company saw a 2% impact on volumes. In my view, the loss of a partner in Poland highlights the risk that InPost faces, besides any volume decline or deceleration in growth due to macroeconomics. The business of automated parcel machines is not easy to enter, but it is also not a high burden. Where InPost can differentiate is through service levels and its existing footprint throughout Europe. Furthermore, we note that while APMs may be relatively easy to deploy, the logistics requirements from collecting to sorting to linehaul are substantially more cash-intensive, and that is something where InPost may have an advantage.

The company generated a PLN 1 billion adjusted EBITDA, and while this marked a 12.6% increase year-on-year, the adjusted margins fell from 33.8% to 28.3%. This was driven by the integration of Yodel. Absent related costs, the margins would have been 35%, driving a 1.2-point improvement. So, the loss of Allegro as a partner has really pressured sentiment in a quarter that was otherwise showing that the company can expand its margins through scale.

InPost Revises Expectations For Poland Downward

For 2025, the company still expects volumes to grow 25% to 30%. However, in Poland, volumes are now expected to be in the high single digits, driven by the business taken away by Allegro. Previously, the company guided for high single-digit growth to low double-digit growth. The company also saw some weakness in e-commerce. In the Eurozone, the company now expects mid-double-digit growth compared to high-single-digit to low-double-digit growth expected earlier. In the UK, volumes are expected to triple.

This translates to 35% to 40% growth in revenues, ahead of volume growth, and 20% to 25% growth in adjusted EBITDA. So, the adjusted EBITDA margin outlook is not really great, with EBITDA growing less than revenues and volumes, but this is driven by integration costs for Yodel.

InPost Stock Upgraded To "Strong Buy"

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

I previously had a $10.44 price target for InPost. I am lowering that now by splitting the price target into a base case target of $8.16, driven by FY25 earnings at a peer group valuation, and $10.53 in a more optimistic scenario, driven by the expected FY26 earnings. So, we see between 32% and 71% upside using the conservative peer group valuation. At the same time, I am upgrading the stock to "Strong Buy" given that the current EV/EBITDA is below the peer group multiple. On EBITDA, I expect annual growth of around 19% and 9.2% on free cash flow. So, the company is expected to substantially improve its earnings and cash flows in the years ahead.

Conclusion: InPost Has Significant Stock Price Growth Ahead

The fallout with Allegro has sent InPost stock prices lower, and combined with weaker e-commerce spending in Poland, that will affect growth in Poland. That remains a watch item. However, I believe the company has shown its ability to grow the business while also growing margins, and it has expanded outside of Poland, with sales outside Poland now representing around half of the sales. The quarter showed that partners can turn into competitors, so it is not a completely high-burden market to enter, but I believe it is also not as easy as putting an automated parcel machine or locker somewhere and earning money. Behind the network of machines, there is an entire network of partners, and InPost also provides sorting, fulfillment, and line-haul preceding the last-mile delivery steps. Setting up that network behind the machines is more costly and should shield InPost somewhat, next to the fact that it is already leading the market with lockers and parcel machines.

data.title: VFV:CA: The Canadian FOMO Quencher
data.description:
Stock picking is not as easy or productive (in terms of getting your daily dose of vitamin C) as fruit picking. It's not even that enjoyable, especially when you end up holding the bag on your pick, while your neighbor hits the jackpot with theirs. Yes, yes, we know, "comparison is the thief of joy", but it is easier said than done. If you:

  • Are you a Canadian going through intense FOMO at the fortunes being made by your neighbors across the pond?
  • Don't have the time or temperament to pick and choose stocks. You want a piece of all the largest 500 companies in the United States.
  • Are bullish on the US dollar, i.e., are stone-cold about the resultant USD exposure.
  • S&P 500 is blazing, and the blacksmith in you wants to strike when the iron is hot.
  • You did not raise an eyebrow, but your pulse noticeably quickened when you read the following headline, and you whispered "carpe diem" feverishly to yourself.

Vanguard S&P 500 Index ETF (TSX:VFV:CA) will not just make you smile; you will beam, my friend. Just take a look at this fund's objective:

.. seeks to track, to the extent reasonably possible and before fees and expenses, the performance of a broad U.S. equity index that measures the investment return of large-capitalization U.S. stocks. Currently, this Vanguard ETF seeks to track the S&P 500 Index (or any successor thereto).

Source: Fund Website

The expenses referred to in the objective are a mere 0.09% on an annual basis (0.08% of it is for management fees). Since indices do not have any expenses, ETFs tracking their performance can only do so, to the extent possible, after their expenses are accounted for. In VFV's case, the expenses are hardly an impediment to achieving its objectives. This passive fund also employs the full replication strategy to achieve the S&P 500 returns.

This strategy essentially involves investing in all the securities of the index in approximately the same proportion as the index. VFV executes this primarily in an indirect way by investing in an affiliated fund.

Vanguard S&P 500 Index ETF invests primarily in a U.S.-domiciled Vanguard Fund that seeks to track the S&P 500 Index. In the alternative or in addition to investing in the Vanguard Fund, the Vanguard S&P 500 Index ETF may invest its assets directly in the stocks that make up the S&P 500 Index.

Source: Prospectus

The affiliated ETF has its own expenses, and its market value reflects that. So one would think that holding another fund would result in VFV indirectly also bearing the expenses of that fund. As is the case with most funds of funds, there is a provision to prevent this duplication of expenses.

To ensure that there is no duplication of management fees chargeable in connection with the Vanguard ETF and its investment in the Underlying Funds managed by the Manager or an affiliate of the Manager, as the case may be, the management fee payable by the Vanguard ETF to the Manager set out above is reduced by the aggregate of the Underlying Fund Management Fee payable by the Underlying Funds to the Manager or an affiliate of the Manager, as the case may be, in connection with the applicable holdings of the Vanguard ETF.

Source: Management Report of Fund Performance

The sector allocation on July 31 gives us a glimpse of VFV's replication strategy in action.

And as expected, the top 10 holdings are populated with behemoths of our time and form over a third of the portfolio.

Several on this list enrich our lives on a daily basis with Mr. AI. We have seen above how VFV goes about implementing its strategy. With nominal expenses, it has also done quite well with its objective of tracking the index. Take a look at the total returns since its inception in 2011.

Most of the large capitalization stocks do not pay huge dividends, so the returns have come primarily by way of price appreciation.

For context, VFV pays on a quarterly basis and currently yields less than a percent.

Outlook & Verdict

The absurdity of owning the S&P 500 via a passive ETF will only be seen in hindsight. Perhaps in a decade or two, investors will finally realize that "hey, owning just based on market capitalization when valuations were at the 99th percentile was probably not the smartest decision." Who needs Dot Com bubble nostalgia when you have an AI bubble?

Why buy a country ETF when you can buy NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA)?

But as expected, the retail crowd buys the most at the top and sells the most at the bottom. Canadians, exhausted with merely good returns at home, have gone all-in through the Southern border.

The true magnitude of this can be seen directly in this ETF's assets.

Compare that to the home team.

When you have more assets in the foreign ETF, you know something is broken in the risk-management chain. With the S&P 500, you are getting your FOMO itch satisfied, just like in the year 2000.

And it will work out swimmingly well, just like the year 2000. This is a strong sell in our books.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: Ciena Q3 Review: Will Continue To Ride The AI Wave
data.description:
Introduction

Ciena Corporation (NYSE:CIEN) just reported its fiscal Q3 results, which investors seemed to love quite a bit. I wanted to go through the numbers in more detail and give some comments on the outlook. Even with some optimistic assumptions, the company is trading at a premium. I maintain a Hold rating.

By the numbers

Starting from the top, sales came in at $1.22B, up an impressive 29.5% y/y, which also beat analysts’ estimates by around $50m. And what moved the company’s sales performance? You’ve guessed it; it was, of course, driven by demand for AI infrastructure and high-speed connectivity. Looking at the revenue breakdown in more detail, we can see that Networking Platforms, which accounted for 77.2% of total revenues, were up 34.6%, and within this segment, Optical Networking came in at over $815 million, with Routing and Switching coming in at $126 million. Global Services grew almost 20% y/y to $160m.

In terms of efficiency and profitability, Q3 non-GAAP EPS came in at $0.67, beating analysts’ estimates by 14 cents. On a GAAP basis, gross margins were down 160 bps compared to the same time last year, while operating margins came in at 6.1%, an increase of 320 bps y/y. We can see that some highlights in operating expenses increased by a lot less or even decreased y/y compared to the company’s revenue growth this quarter, which helped its operating margins improve by so much.

A commendable job by the management to manage costs effectively and improve profitability significantly y/y.

What about the company’s financial situation? CIEN finished the quarter with over $1.3B in cash, equivalents, and short-term investments, against $1.5B in long-term debt. Is that worrisome? Looking at the company’s interest coverage ratio for the quarter, we can see it’s around 3.2x, which is above what many analysts consider healthy (2x). So, 3.2x coverage coupled with a decent cash pile, I don’t think it is a risk to have that much debt on its books right now.

On top of these results, the company provided forward guidance for the next quarter, which seems like the market loved, sending the company’s shares soaring over 20% on the market open.

The management is forecasting the company will do around $1.24B-$1.32B ($1.28B at the midpoint) in sales, which is well above the consensus expectations of $1.21B. The management is attributing this outlook to the strength in AI and high-speed connectivity solutions, which I mentioned earlier, that is driving record-large order backlogs.

Overall, I’d say a double beat on performance as well as above expectations of forward guidance estimates, the company couldn’t have asked for any better result. I don’t think that these results should have pushed the company’s share price so much on the day, though. They were good, but nothing stands out as extraordinary in my view. Such a price performance should have been accompanied by a much bigger beat and a raise in guidance, as well as revenue growth to be close to 50% or 60%.

Comments on the Outlook

The management expects to see around 17% growth in revenues for fiscal 2026, with gross margins to be in the range of 42% to 44%. These aren’t too impressive in my view; however, what is a lot more impressive is the fact that the management expects the company to be a lot more profitable. The management expects the operating margin for next year to be around 16%, which was raised by 100 bps from previously. This is an amazing improvement in operations, which surely helped the company’s share price this morning. I often say that top-line growth is good to have; however, I like to focus on the company’s internal operation more, which means I like to see margin expansion. This sort of margin improvement, which I believe is in non-GAAP terms, is an amazing accomplishment nonetheless, and I do hope it will be achieved over the next year. High-margin areas like AI-driven networking and cloud infrastructure are helping the company to become a lot more profitable in the future, which translates to around a 45% improvement in operations if we take the company’s latest non-GAAP operating margin profile of 10.7%.

This tells me that the management is laser-focused on capitalizing on the robust demand for essentially anything AI-related, and this shift in focus is the way to go. In my opinion, this kind of margin uplift is rather impressive for a capital-intensive company like CIEN, which operates in a very competitive tech environment, where there are many companies that may offer a similar AI-driven product. So, the fact that the company is looking to expand margins significantly over the next year tells us that CIEN is the preferred vendor for such tech.

So, what is the management saying about keeping the momentum going? How is the company going to maintain and increase margins in the future? The target for next year is around 15.5% at the midpoint in terms of operating margins. The management is saying that there is a lot of potential for continuing to accelerate in the future. The company’s CFO, Mark Graff, said:

“We believe that we will accelerate our longer-term goal of 15% to 16% operating margin by one year from 2027 to 2026, driven by increased operating leverage and improving gross margins.”

Here, he is referring to the company’s ability to continue to innovate its portfolio through increases in R&D to bring further operational efficiencies, as well as the rapid growth they see in the market.

Furthermore, the CEO, Gary Smith, said he is also confident that there will be a lot more margin expansion in the company over the next while, citing the “accelerated demand from AI workloads and improving service provider investments,” underscoring the company’s shift towards software and AI-driven solutions, which have been known to have a much higher-margin profile than anything else these days. The transition from hardware-centric to a software and services-rich business mix is certainly the way to improve margins substantially. It would be great to have some numbers, but that progress will be evident in the future quarters.

Valuation

I thought it would be beneficial to see what kind of performance we can expect from the current expectations in terms of operations. I am also being quite optimistic here, so take the assumptions with a grain of salt.

My valuation assumptions will look at the company’s following 5-year transformation into what I am assuming is going to be a lot more profitable due to the shift in focus, as we mentioned, so the below are my assumptions, which include around 20% CAGR in sales, 20% operating margins, a discount rate of around 8.7%, which is the company’s WACC, as well as a 2.5% terminal growth rate. As you can see, these are not conservative at all, but just bear with me.

So, what is the fair value with these assumptions? It’s around $101 a share, which means the company’s current valuation may be somewhat stretched.

Closing Comments

I am going to maintain my Hold rating from before, because the company needs to achieve quite a lot more profitability and even sales growth acceleration before I would consider it a good investment. I think the company will continue to ride the AI tailwinds for a bit longer, but if the management cannot improve the company a lot more than it has already planned, I don’t think it is wise to chase AI gains for now.

data.title: Diamond Hill International Strategy Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Commentary

The second quarter saw another strong performance from international stocks, with the MSCI ACWI ex-US Index increasing 12.03%. Despite this solid return, markets experienced notable volatility driven by geopolitical events. Liberation Day initially triggered a sharp sell-off, reflecting investor anxiety over the possibility of severe tariffs. However, markets quickly rebounded as many of the proposed tariffs were delayed or settled at rates much lower than the initial headlines. Meanwhile, tensions stemming from the Israel-Iran conflict had significant humanitarian and geopolitical impacts, causing a brief spike in oil prices; however, markets remained resilient overall.

Europe led the benchmark higher, supported by rate cuts and easing inflation expectations. NATO’s collective decision to increase defense spending to 5% of GDP, along with the UK’s upcoming infrastructure investment plans, offered optimism despite challenges such as aging infrastructure, as illustrated by recent grid issues in Spain. In Japan, more signs of corporate governance reform were evident through increased share buybacks and the consolidation of parent-child listings, despite ongoing concerns about future GDP growth. Chinese equities mostly stayed flat, reflecting continued uncertainty about the outlook for consumer demand. Improved political stability after South Korea’s presidential election boosted its local equity market. Meanwhile, in Latin America, rate cuts and a stronger peso helped lift Mexican stocks, and talks of budget discipline combined with cheap valuations pushed Brazilian stocks higher.

Performance Discussion

Our portfolio delivered a solid absolute return this quarter, although it slightly underperformed the benchmark. The consumer staples and industrials sectors were positive contributors, while information technology and consumer discretionary detracted on a relative basis. Geographically, holdings in France and Japan boosted relative performance, whereas holdings in Canada and South Korea detracted.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC)(TSM), the world’s leading semiconductor foundry, achieved strong financial performance driven by increased demand for its advanced semiconductor technologies from sectors like machine learning and cloud computing.

Tesco (OTCPK:TSCDF), the largest grocery retailer in the UK, reported solid quarterly results, despite recent market concerns sparked by competitor Asda’s aggressive pricing strategy. Tesco reaffirmed its position as the leading everyday low-price grocery chain in the UK, which supported its share performance.

Mitsubishi Shokuhin (OTCPK:MSHXF), a major food distributor in Japan, rose sharply following its announced buyout by Mitsubishi Corp., reflecting ongoing improvements in corporate governance and parent-child listing consolidations in Japan, an area of focus for us.

Conversely, Bunzl (OTCPK:BZLFF), a global distributor of non-food consumable products, faced an unexpected quarterly earnings shortfall due to execution issues in its North American operations. Despite this short-term setback, we remain confident in the management team and their proven execution capabilities.

Alibaba (BABA), China’s largest e-commerce and tech giant, experienced slow consumer spending and weaker-than- expected e-commerce growth, worsened by concerns about China’s economic recovery and increased geopolitical tensions.

Gerresheimer (OTCPK:GRRMF), a leading global manufacturer of specialty glass and plastic packaging for pharmaceuticals and cosmetics, lowered its 2025 guidance, citing weak cosmetics demand and temporary softness in medical packaging. We expect organic growth to bounce back later this year, driven by increasing demand for higher-value injectable drug packaging solutions.

Portfolio Activity

The volatility this quarter enabled us to add three new stocks that we had been monitoring closely when their share prices became attractive. At the same time, we decided to exit six positions, choosing to sell two stocks where our investment thesis had been fulfilled and to exit several other positions where our preference was to own other stocks where we had higher conviction.

We initiated a position in Ireland’s leading retail bank, Allied Irish Bank (AIB). Ireland has a favorable economic backdrop, and AIB holds a dominant market share in a highly consolidated banking sector. The bank’s solid capital position enables the potential for strong returns to shareholders through dividends and buybacks, with further upside expected following the recent sale of the Irish government’s final stake in the bank. We believe the market underestimates the bank’s market position and its potential for capital returns.

Hindustan Aeronautics is India’s leading aerospace and defense company, offering essential services such as the design, manufacturing and maintenance of military aircraft and helicopters. The company’s large order backlog is driven by India’s strategic move toward defense self-reliance and higher defense spending. Short-term market concerns from operational setbacks created an attractive entry point into this fundamentally strong business with clear long-term earnings prospects.

BAWAG Bank (OTCPK:BWAGF), a leading retail bank in Austria, operates a highly efficient digital banking model that supports sustained profitability and has a proven record of shareholder-friendly capital allocation. The bank maintains a notably low-cost structure and delivers strong returns. We took advantage of temporary market declines related to trade tensions to establish our position at an attractive discount to intrinsic value.

We sold several positions during the quarter. Notably, our exit from Mitsubishi Shokuhin occurred after the company announced a buyout by Mitsubishi Corp. Since Mitsubishi Corp. already held a significant stake in Mitsubishi Shokuhin, acquiring the remaining shares consolidated the parent and subsidiary under the same company. Although this event was not central to our investment thesis, we saw it as a potential source of embedded optionality when we established our position in 2024.

We also exited our position in the Belgian pharmaceutical company UCB S.A. UCB was a notable contributor to performance during our holding period, and its stock price had reached a point where the discount to intrinsic value had narrowed. Additionally, with uncertainty regarding potential pharmaceutical tariffs, we believed it was wise to exit the position given the combination of tariff risks and a narrower discount to intrinsic value. Lastly, we exited positions in Capstone Copper (CS:CA), Prosus NV (OTCPK:PROSY), Arcos Dorados (ARCO) and Nestlé (OTCPK:NSRGY), where we preferred other positions in the portfolio where conviction was higher.

Market Outlook

International stocks have posted strong returns so far this year, yet they still present attractive relative valuations compared to US equities. While there isn’t a single dominant theme across all non-US markets, different regions and countries offer compelling reasons for optimism about their future prospects, potentially unlocking value through economic and corporate developments.

Europe’s increased spending on defense and infrastructure, Japan’s ongoing corporate governance reforms and China’s complex economic and corporate landscape each present unique opportunities and risks. Tariff negotiations have also significantly contributed to market volatility in recent months, and we humbly acknowledge that predicting these outcomes is beyond our skill set. There have been meaningful market swings over just a few months, underscoring the importance of remaining grounded in our investment approach.

In this environment, we continue to follow our disciplined investment philosophy, focusing on companies where a gap exists between the intrinsic value of the business and the current stock price. By staying flexible and nimble in our approach to discovering new ideas, we seek to capture value across diverse international markets while maintaining careful risk management to achieve sustainable, long-term returns for our investors.

data.title: Universal Corp: Diversification Driving Our Buy Call
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Nightbloom Analysis as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Introduction

Universal Corporation (NYSE:UVV) is a global leader in the leaf tobacco industry, operating across more than 30 countries. Universal sources and processes tobacco and other plant-based ingredients for consumer product manufacturers and benefits from the global supply chain and existing relationships with major consumer goods companies. Because of that, UVV is a critical player in the agricultural products sector, especially in the tobacco supply chain.

Over the next 1-3 years, Universal's outlook is shaped by two strategic drivers. First, Tobacco Operations are positioned for margin stability as supply-demand conditions normalize, which should support steady cash generation and dividend coverage. Second, the Ingredients segment offers structural growth potential as new capacity and acquisitions ramp up, diversifying revenues and unlocking operating leverage. The stock price has been flat year-over-year, which may not be in line with these developments and indicates that the market isn't fully appreciating this company's potential.

The Company In A Nutshell

Universal Corporation operates through two primary segments.

Tobacco Operations

This segment sources, processes, and sells leaf tobacco to the world's largest cigarette manufacturers, such as Philip Morris International Inc. (PM), Japan Tobacco, Inc. (OTCPK:JAPAY), and Imperial Brands PLC (IMMBY). One of the company’s biggest strengths is working directly with farmers. The main tobacco varieties handled are flue-cured, burley, and oriental, which are diversified by region and end-use. The company processes the leaves to customer specifications, manages quality control, and delivers them. Even though this segment has declined, it is still the backbone of the company, as it generates more than 75% of revenue.

Ingredients Operations

Universal has made several strategic moves to diversify into plant-based ingredients. They did this through acquisitions such as Silva International (dehydrated fruits and vegetables), Shank's Extracts (vanilla and other natural flavors), and FruitSmart (fruit juices and concentrates). Through this segment, different food, beverage, and flavor companies are customers that use those products in packaged foods, beverages, and more. Universal’s strategy is to use the tobacco model to source crops, process them at scale, and provide logistics. The Ingredients Operations isn't contributing that much to revenues yet, but it is expanding and has a higher long-term margin potential.

What Recent Results Tell Us

Universal reported its first quarter results over the period ending June 30, 2025, with revenue of $594 million. This is a slight decrease compared to the previous quarter. However, the company achieved a significant change in profitability, with operating income rising to $34 million from $17 million. Net income was $8.5 million, or $0.34 per diluted share.

The main driver of performance was the Tobacco Operations segment, which generated $504.7 million in revenue. The revenue of the segment is down 8% y-o-y because of a decrease in carryover crop sales. However, due to a favorable product mix in Asia and improved pricing strategies, operating income for the segment rose significantly. This mix was driven by higher demand for flue-cured leaf from African and Asian suppliers, which has higher margins than burley tobacco. The sales, particularly to Japan Tobacco and other Chinese buyers, helped offset weaker burley volumes in North America.

On the other hand, the Ingredients Operations segment saw a decline in operating income due to a less favorable product mix and tariff uncertainties. This creates a short-term drag on margins, with two broader implications. First, these pressures highlight the execution risk that comes with bringing in a new business line, where external factors can weigh on profitability, such as trade policy. Second, these margin pressures could slow the revenue growth of the Ingredients segment.

From a valuation standpoint, this creates a short-term cap on multiple expansion. The market is probably discounting the growth of Ingredients until the company shows consistent profitability. Investors are cautious as margins are under pressure from tariffs and higher costs. Until the company demonstrates sustained profitability in the segment, the market will keep discounting the growth. However, with successfully resolving tariff uncertainties or improving the product mix, the trajectory could shift, which supports stronger revenues.

On the balance sheet, cash increased by $76 million quarter-over-quarter, which led to a cash balance of $178 million. Long-term debt is at $618 million, which results in a debt-to-equity ratio of 41%. Even with elevated leverage, the company could still meet interest obligations from operating income. The balance sheet shows adequate near-term liquidity and great interest coverage, offset by substantial leverage, which could negatively affect the financial flexibility if crop conditions or global demand were to weaken.

In terms of cash flow, the company continues to reflect the seasonal nature of the tobacco business. Regularly, free cash flow is often negative in the first half of the fiscal year, as the company’s focus is on building inventory and extending working capital to suppliers. As expected, this was the case for Q1 too, where working capital requirements absorbed a big part of operating cash. Management typically offsets this cyclicality with credit facilities, which is the reason for the high debt levels. That said, Universal has always converted earnings into positive free cash flow over the full year, especially in the second half of the year. In that period, crop deliveries will accelerate, which will lead to receivables being converted to cash. The business model has proven capable of generating enough annual cash to cover dividends and service debt.

Two Paths Ahead: Tobacco vs. Ingredients

Universal’s management gave clear directional guidance for fiscal year 2026. The company is expecting sales of around $1.35-1.45 billion, which is 2-9% lower than in the fiscal year 2025. Also, it is expected to see tobacco move to a more balanced position as crop size will increase by 25-45% over the fiscal year. With current crop sizes, flue-cured and burley tobacco will be in oversupply positions by the end of the fiscal year. Flue-cured tobacco is commonly used in cigarette blends, which make up the biggest portion of the volumes. Burley, on the other hand, is a lighter leaf that adds specific flavor notes. Both of them are core revenue drivers, which means that oversupply could directly pressure their pricing. Management also stated that the continued progress will support existing and new Universal Ingredients customers with the resources and expanded facilities.

The acceleration of the plant-based ingredients platform is very important. The company is building scale with acquisitions, which indicates a real move to leverage processing and logistics skills in higher-margin and growing markets. Universal offsets the decline in tobacco demand while opening potential in promising markets. Universal could capture a meaningful slice of the growing demand for natural flavors and plant-based ingredients with its existing processing and logistics expertise. Revenue streams will be diversified, profitability will improve, and reliance on tobacco will decline.

Another important factor of the expansion is the diversification and leveraging of existing farming and logistics capabilities. Looking at sweet potatoes as an example, Universal can apply the same sourcing, processing, and logistics model proven in tobacco to ingredients. Management has already highlighted sweet potatoes and other specialty crops as examples where its sourcing and processing model can be replicated, a strategy reinforced by recent acquisitions in plant-based ingredients. This model includes direct farmer contracting, agronomic support, and centralized processing before distribution. Universal could ensure quality and consistency by applying this model to sweet potatoes while still scaling production. This then reduces the exposure to the decline of tobacco. Importantly, this strategy aligns with the broader consumer and food industry trends towards natural ingredients, suggesting that Universal's capabilities give it a credible path to grow outside its core market.

In the Tobacco Operations segment, ongoing market normalization could rebalance supply and demand. This would lead to more stable margins if volume management and the product mix remain well executed. The company therefore has to keep inventories aligned with demand and secure contracts with buyers on good terms. Also, the company has to adjust sourcing between flue-cured and burley in line with demand, which leads to stabilized revenues and protected margins. With the Ingredients segment, Universal could benefit from expanded capacity and a broader product portfolio. The segment has potential for stronger operating leverage and margin gains. These dynamics show the company is well-positioned to safeguard and strengthen its competitive standing.

What Could Go Wrong?

Next to these promising developments within its Tobacco segment, there are a few headwinds, too. The possible tobacco oversupply could put pressure on pricing power and margins over time. Also, regulatory or demand shocks could strengthen the decline of tobacco, which reduces cash flow that supports dividends.

On the Ingredients segment side, there are risks to consider as well. Think about the tariff-related uncertainty, but also the elevated fixed costs due to the expansion. With its acquisitions, operations need to be integrated across geographies, cultures, and product lines. Pressure on margins could be the case if there are problems maintaining quality, harmonizing systems, or scaling the production efficiently. If leverage increases, financial flexibility will be reduced if crop conditions deteriorate or cash generation falls below expectations. If these risks were to negatively affect margins or slow down the growth of Ingredients, the valuation discount would move more towards a Hold stance.

Putting Valuation in Perspective

The financial position looks solid with a Debt/EBITDA ratio of 3.7x, which means moderate leverage while still maintaining operational resilience. Also, the company is delivering consistent capital returns, with a dividend yield of around 5.8% and a payout ratio of ~79%. This reinforces investor confidence in the company’s cash generation capabilities.

With regard to valuation metrics, the company is currently trading at an EV/EBITDA of 7.87x, which is well below the 5-year historical median of 9.02x. It is also below the sector median of 12.21x, which is a positive sign, too. The P/E ratio is at 13.5x, which is around the 5-year average but significantly lower than the 10-year average of 17.6x. This suggests that the stock is currently priced with caution, even with stable fundamentals. Historically, when the company last traded at such valuation levels, the company had more leverage, fewer shareholder return programs, and less diversified revenue streams. Today, the company is strengthened by the expanding and diversifying plant-based ingredients operations, robust free cash flow generation, and a great income profile. The company continues to focus on expanding the Ingredients segment, which supports a long-term valuation argument with the sustainable dividend and financial discipline.

The company’s free cash flow yield over the full year (FY 2025) stands at an impressive 18.8%, with $259 million in free cash flow over the whole year and $1.4 billion in market cap. This is significantly higher than the 5-year average of ~2%. The yield shows how the company is improving its cash generation capabilities, especially in the current market environment.

In terms of profitability, the company had an EBITDA margin of above 10%. This is also a great number, but a bit lower than the sector median. However, this is not as simple as that, as Universal is not a pure tobacco player anymore, which means it can not be compared to 1 sector only. The numbers show Universal’s operational efficiency and strategic positioning within the sector.

Why UVV Is a Buy

Universal Corporation is well-positioned for the medium/long term, combining the Tobacco Operations segment with a rapidly expanding Ingredients segment. The company has a lot of experience and knowledge about sourcing, processing, and logistics. This expertise can be leveraged to capture growth in plant-based ingredients and natural flavors. With the move to reduce reliance on tobacco, revenue streams will diversify. Universal is strategically investing in expanding capacity and operational efficiency, which strengthens its competitive edge. With these developments, Universal is aiming for sustainable profitability, enhancement of margins, and long-term shareholder value. The current valuation offers a reasonable and attractive value proposition for investors, as there is low leverage, strong cash flow, and a diverse business mix. This supports a Buy recommendation.
